meta.title: Interpretability Crash Course: Hands-On with TransformerLens, SAEs & Circuits
meta.description: Six-module interactive companion covering the full mechanistic interpretability toolkit — from loading models to replicating the Bloom filter heads paper
meta.author: Peter Balogh

children.children: This is a hands-on crash course in mechanistic interpretability — the tools, techniques, and intuitions you need to understand what's happening inside transformer models. Each module is a runnable Python script with real code, real outputs, and real insights.

Throughout all six modules, we connect back to the Bloom filter heads paper — attention heads that implement probabilistic set-membership testing. By the end, you'll have replicated the full methodology.
The six modules build on each other:
TransformerLens Fundamentals — loading models, hook points, caching activations
Activation Patching & Ablation — causal interventions to test what heads do
SAELens & Sparse Autoencoders — decomposing superposition into interpretable features
Probing & Steering — reading from and writing to internal representations
Circuit Analysis — QK/OV framework, DLA, composition, path patching
Full Bloom Filter Replication — end-to-end paper methodology

TransformerLens (by Neel Nanda) is the standard tool for mechanistic interpretability. It wraps HuggingFace models with 'hook points' at every internal computation, letting you inspect and modify activations mid-forward-pass.

Imagine a telephone exchange where every wire has a tap point. You can listen in on any conversation (read activations) or inject your own signal (modify activations). TransformerLens does this for every computation inside the transformer — queries, keys, values, attention patterns, MLP outputs, residual stream states.
Loading a model is one line. The key insight is what comes back:

props.title: Loading a model and inspecting its structure

children.children:
model = HookedTransformer.from_pretrained("gpt2-small")

print(f"Layers: {model.cfg.n_layers}")           # 12
print(f"Heads per layer: {model.cfg.n_heads}")    # 12  
print(f"Head dimension: {model.cfg.d_head}")      # 64
print(f"Model dimension: {model.cfg.d_model}")    # 768

# Total attention heads: 12 layers × 12 heads = 144 heads
# Each head has dimension 64; 12 × 64 = 768 = d_model
The workhorse method is run_with_cache — it runs a forward pass AND saves every intermediate activation:

children:
prompt = "The doctor examined the patient and the doctor prescribed medicine"
tokens = model.to_tokens(prompt)
logits, cache = model.run_with_cache(tokens)

print(f"Logits shape: {logits.shape}")  # [batch

, seq_len, vocab_size]
print(f"Total cached tensors: {len(cache)}")  # hundreds of tensors
In this attention pattern tensor, the batch dimension indexes the separate input examples being processed simultaneously

through the model. Each example in the batch produces its own full set of attention patterns, so a batch size of 8 means you get 8 independent sets of [head, dest, src] attention maps. When analyzing circuits, you'll often index into a specific batch element (e.g., `pattern[0]`) to inspect how the model attends on one particular input.

props.title: Q: Is this referring to a specific scenario in which we're experimenting by feeding groups of text-segments into the model?

children.children: Yes, exactly. When you work with a transformer model, you typically don't feed in one sentence at a time — you feed in a batch of several inputs at once. This isn't just an experimental choice, it's how GPU hardware works efficiently. Instead of processing "The cat sat on the mat" alone, you might feed in 8 different sentences together as a single batch, and the model processes all 8 in parallel.

In the context of circuit analysis, this matters because you often want to compare how the model behaves across different inputs. For example, you might construct a batch where each example tests a slightly different pattern — maybe varying which noun an indirect object refers to, or swapping gendered pronouns — so you can see how the attention heads respond differently. The batch dimension in your tensor is just the index that lets you pick out which of those examples you're looking at.

So when the passage says you might do pattern[0], that's you saying "show me the full attention map for the first sentence I fed in." pattern[1] would be the second sentence, and so on. Each one has its own complete set of attention patterns across all heads, completely independent of the others. The model doesn't mix information between batch elements — they just happen to ride through the hardware together for speed.
Asked about "input examples being processed..." — 2/27/2026, 1:23:55 PM
Every computation has a named hook point. The critical ones:
blocks.{layer}.attn.hook_pattern — attention patterns [batch, head, dest, src]. WHERE each head attends.
blocks.{layer}.attn.hook_result — attention output [batch, seq, head, d_head]. WHAT each head writes.
blocks.{layer}.attn.hook_q / hook_k / hook_v — query, key, value vectors.
blocks.{layer}.hook_resid_pre / hook_resid_post — residual stream before/after the layer.
blocks.{layer}.mlp.hook_post — MLP layer output.
The naming convention is systematic: blocks.{layer}.{component}.hook_{activation}. Once you know the pattern, you can tap into any computation.
With the cache in hand, finding Bloom filter head signatures is straightforward — check which heads attend strongly when a token repeats:

props.title: Finding heads that attend to repeated tokens

children:
# Find positions of repeated token
token_strs = [model.to_string(t) for t in tokens[0]]
doctor_positions = [i for i, s in enumerate(token_strs) if "doctor" in s.lower()]
first_doc, second_doc = doctor_positions[0], doctor_positions[1]

# Check every head in every layer
for layer in range(model.cfg.n_layers):
    attn = cache[f"blocks.{layer}.attn.hook_pattern"]
    for head in range(model.cfg.n_heads):
        attn_to_first

= attn[0, head, second_doc, first_doc].item()
        if attn_to_first > 0.1:  # Only show heads with significant attention
            print(f"  L{layer}H{head}: {attn_to_first:.3f} attention")

props.title: Q: This variable name seems to imply something, but I'm not sure what. How do we interpret this code?

children.children: attn_to_first is the attention weight that the second occurrence of "doctor" pays to the first occurrence of "doctor." The name literally means "attention to the first one" — first referring to the first doctor token's position in the sequence.

Here is how to read the line that computes it: attn[0, head, second_doc, first_doc]. The attention pattern tensor has shape [batch, head, destination, source]. So index 0 is the batch dimension, head selects which attention head we are examining, second_doc is the destination position (the token that is "looking"), and first_doc is the source position (the token being "looked at"). The resulting scalar tells you what fraction of the second doctor token's attention budget this head spends on the first doctor token.

Think of it like a student in a classroom. The second "doctor" token is a student trying to decide who to listen to. Each attention head gives that student a different strategy. attn_to_first measures how much one particular head's strategy says "pay attention to the earlier place in the text where this same word appeared." A value near 1.0 means that head is almost entirely focused on the first occurrence; a value near 0 means it ignores it.

The loop checks this value across every layer and every head, filtering for cases where it exceeds some threshold. This is how you discover "duplicate token heads" or "previous token heads" — heads that have learned the specific circuit of noticing when a token has appeared before and routing information from the first occurrence to the second. That is why the variable name is so telling: the whole point of this code is to find heads whose job is attending to the first instance of a repeated token.
Asked about "attn_to_first" — 2/27/2026, 1:26:19 PM
Running the full selectivity analysis across multiple sentences gives results like:

Head       Hit Attn     Baseline     Selectivity 
----------------------------------------------
L0H1      0.3241       0.0034       95.3x
L0H5      0.2187       0.0089       24.6x
L1H11     0.1854       0.0062       29.9x
L3H0      0.1432       0.0041       34.9x

💡 Heads with selectivity >30x and hit attention >0.1
   are your Bloom filter head candidates!

Ablation is the causal hammer of interpretability. Instead of just observing what a head does, you knock it out and see what breaks. This tells you what the head is for, not just what it correlates with.

This is exactly what neuroscientists do with lesion studies. If you damage Broca's area and the patient can't produce speech, you know Broca's area is causally involved in speech production. We're doing the same thing to transformer components — knock it out, see what breaks.

props.title: Zero ablation: zeroing out a head's output

children.children:
def zero_ablation_hook(value, hook, head_idx):
    """Zero out one head's output in the attention result."""
    # value shape: [batch, seq, n_heads, d_head]
    value[:, :, head_idx, :] = 0.0
    return value

# Ablate L0H1 and see what changes
layer, head = 0, 1
ablated_logits = model.run_with_hooks(
    clean_tokens,
    fwd_hooks=[(
        f"blocks.{layer}.attn.hook_result",
        lambda value, hook: zero_ablation_hook(value, hook, head)
    )]
)

props.title: Zero vs Mean vs Activation Patching

children.children: There are three levels of sophistication:
Zero ablation sets a head's output to zero. It's simple but has a problem: zero is out-of-distribution for downstream layers — they've never seen exactly-zero input from a head during training.

Mean ablation replaces the head's output with its average across many inputs. This is more principled — it asks 'what if this head contributed nothing beyond its average?' Downstream layers see something within their expected distribution.

Activation patching is the gold standard. You run the model on two inputs (clean vs corrupted) and swap activations from one run into the other at specific points. If patching head H's output from clean→corrupted changes the answer, that head carries the critical information.

props.title: Activation patching between clean and corrupted runs

children.children:
clean_prompt = "The doctor examined the patient and the doctor prescribed"
corrupted_prompt = "The doctor examined the patient and the nurse prescribed"

_, clean_cache = model.run_with_cache(model.to_tokens(clean_prompt))
_, corrupted_cache = model.run_with_cache(model.to_tokens(corrupted_prompt))

# Patch: run corrupted input, but replace this head's output with clean
for layer in range(model.cfg.n_layers):
    for head in range(model.cfg.n_heads):
        clean_head_output = clean_cache[f"blocks.{layer}.attn.hook_result"]
        
        def patch_hook(value, hook, h=head, clean_out=clean_head_output):
            value[:, :, h, :] = clean_out[:, :, h, :]
            return value
        
        patched_logits = model.run_with_hooks(
            corrupted_tokens,
            fwd_hooks=[(f"blocks.{layer}.attn.hook_result", patch_hook)]
        )
The Bloom filter ablation experiment is the definitive causal test:

Condition            Clean PPL    Ablated PPL    Δ%        
--------------------------------------------------------
Repeat tokens        23.45        25.73          +9.7%
Novel tokens         31.22        29.85          -4.4%

Interaction (repeat Δ - novel Δ): +14.1%

💡 Paper results: repeat +9.7%, novel -4.4%, interaction +14.1%
   Bloom heads specifically help with repeats and HURT novel processing
   (through false positives — exactly what the Bloom filter model predicts)

Sparse Autoencoders solve the superposition problem — the fact that individual neurons respond to many unrelated concepts. SAEs decompose activations into a much larger set of monosemantic features, each (ideally) corresponding to one interpretable concept.

props.title: Superposition: Why Single Neurons Aren't Interpretable

children.children: A single neuron in a transformer might activate for the Golden Gate Bridge, the number 7, French cuisine, and something else entirely. This is called superposition: the model stores more concepts than it has neurons by using overlapping, distributed representations.
It's efficient for the model but terrible for interpretability. SAEs fix this by learning a much larger dictionary of features, enforcing sparsity so only a handful fire on any given input.
Architecture: activations (768d) → encoder → features (65536d, sparse) → decoder → reconstructed (768d). Training minimizes reconstruction error + sparsity penalty.

White light looks like one thing, but a prism decomposes it into a spectrum of pure colors. SAEs are prisms for neural network activations — they decompose a 'white' activation vector into its component 'colors' (monosemantic features). Each feature is a pure concept, and the original activation is a mixture of these concepts.

props.title: Loading and using a pretrained SAE

children.children:
from sae_lens import SAE

# Load pretrained SAE for GPT-2 small, layer 0 residual stream
sae, cfg_dict, sparsity = SAE.from_pretrained(
    release="gpt2-small-res-jb",
    sae_id="blocks.0.hook_resid_pre",
)

print(f"Input dimension (d_model): {sae.cfg.d_in}")    # 768
print(f"Feature dimension (d_sae): {sae.cfg.d_sae}")   # 24576
print(f"Expansion factor: {sae.cfg.d_sae / sae.cfg.d_in:.0f}x")  # 32x

# Run activations through the SAE encoder
_, cache = model.run_with_cache(tokens)
activations = cache["blocks.0.hook_resid_pre"]
feature_acts = sae.encode(activations)  # [batch, seq, d_sae]

Non-zero features per token position:
  0: '<BOS>'  → 47 active features out of 24576
  1: 'The'    → 62 active features out of 24576
  2: ' Golden' → 89 active features out of 24576
  3: ' Gate'  → 71 active features out of 24576

Average active features per position: 67
Sparsity: 0.27% of features active
For the Bloom filter connection, the key question is: are there SAE features that fire specifically when a token is repeated?

repeat_prompts = [
    ("The doctor examined the patient and the doctor prescribed medicine", "doctor"),
    ("The cat sat on the mat and the cat purred loudly", "cat"),
    ("A bird flew over the lake and the bird landed nearby", "bird"),
]

first_occurrence_acts = []
second_occurrence_acts = []

for prompt, word in repeat_prompts:
    tokens = model.to_tokens(prompt)
    _, cache = model.run_with_cache(tokens)
    features = sae.encode(cache["blocks.0.hook_resid_pre"])
    
    token_strs = [model.to_string(t) for t in tokens[0]]
    word_positions = [i for i, t in enumerate(token_strs) if word.lower() in t.lower()]
    
    if len(word_positions) >= 2:
        first_occurrence_acts.append(features[0, word_positions[0]])
        second_occurrence_acts.append(features[0, word_positions[1]])

# Features more active on second occurrence = repetition detectors
avg_first = torch.stack(first_occurrence_acts).mean(dim=0)
avg_second = torch.stack(second_occurrence_acts).mean(dim=0)
repeat_specific = avg_second - avg_first
top_repeat_features = torch.topk(repeat_specific, 10)

The Bloom filter heads we identified at the attention-head level might be implementing the computation that these SAE repetition features detect. This bridges head-level analysis (our paper) with feature-level analysis (Anthropic's Scaling Monosemanticity work).

Probing asks 'Is information X encoded in the model's activations at layer L?' Steering asks 'Can I change the model's behavior by adding a direction to its activations?' Together they form a read/write interface to internal representations.

props.title: Reading and Writing the Model's Mind

children.children: A linear probe is like a thermometer — it reads a single quantity (temperature / 'has this token been seen before?') from a complex system without disturbing it. A steering vector is like turning up the thermostat — you inject a signal that changes the system's behavior in a targeted way. Together: read, then write.

props.title: Training a linear probe for repetition detection

children.children: Probes can find information the model DOESN'T USE. Just because repetition info is encoded at layer 3 doesn't mean the model actually uses it. This is why ablation is the gold standard for causal claims — probing is correlational. Always pair probes with ablation experiments.
Steering vectors are the write side. Compute a direction in activation space that corresponds to 'repeated token' and add it during inference:

props.title: Computing and applying a steering vector

children.children:
# Collect activations for repeated vs novel token positions
mean_repeated = torch.stack(repeated_acts).mean(dim=0)
mean_novel = torch.stack(novel_acts).mean(dim=0)

# The steering vector: direction from novel → repeated
steering_vector = mean_repeated - mean_novel
steering_vector = steering_vector / steering_vector.norm()

# Apply during inference
def steering_hook(value, hook, sv=steering_vector, alpha=10.0):
    value[:, -1, :] += alpha * sv
    return value

steered_logits = model.run_with_hooks(
    test_tokens,
    fwd_hooks=[(f"blocks.{steering_layer}.hook_resid_post", steering_hook)]
)

This is the theoretical backbone: the 'Mathematical Framework for Transformer Circuits' (Elhage et al., 2021). The key insight is that each attention head has TWO independent circuits — the QK circuit determines WHERE the head attends, and the OV circuit determines WHAT it copies.

props.title: QK/OV Circuits: The WHERE and WHAT of Attention

children.children: Every attention head has four weight matrices: W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O. These combine into two independent circuits:
QK circuit: W_Q^T × W_K (d_model × d_model). Determines the attention pattern — 'Token at position i attends to position j if the QK score is high.' This is WHERE information flows.

OV circuit: W_V × W_O (d_model × d_model). Determines what happens when attention fires — 'When head attends from i to j, it reads the residual stream at j, multiplies by OV, and writes to i.' This is WHAT information moves.

The beauty: you can analyze these independently. A head that detects repeated tokens (QK) might copy the token identity (OV) or might write a generic 'this was repeated' signal (OV) — same QK, different OV.

# Extract QK circuit for a candidate Bloom filter head
W_Q = model.W_Q[layer, head]   # [d_model, d_head]
W_K = model.W_K[layer, head]   # [d_model, d_head]
QK_circuit = W_Q @ W_K.T       # [d_model, d_model]

# Test: do same-token pairs get higher QK scores?
embeds = model.W_E[test_token_ids]  # token embeddings
queries = embeds @ W_Q
keys = embeds @ W_K
qk_scores = queries @ keys.T / (model.cfg.d_head ** 0.5)

# Compare diagonal (same token) vs off-diagonal (different token)
diag_mean = qk_scores.diag().mean().item()
offdiag_mean = qk_scores[~torch.eye(len(test_token_ids), dtype=bool)].mean().item()
selectivity = diag_mean - offdiag_mean

props.title: QK selectivity of Bloom filter heads

children.children:
L0H1:  same-token QK = +2.34, diff-token QK = -0.18, selectivity = +2.52
L0H5:  same-token QK = +1.87, diff-token QK = +0.12, selectivity = +1.75
L1H11: same-token QK = +2.01, diff-token QK = -0.31, selectivity = +2.32
L3H0:  same-token QK = +1.65, diff-token QK = +0.08, selectivity = +1.57

💡 Heads with high selectivity have QK circuits that specifically
   detect token repetition — this is the MECHANISM, not just a correlation.

props.title: Path Patching: The Gold Standard for Circuit Discovery

children.children: Path patching asks: 'If I replace a specific activation on a specific path with its value from a different input, how does the output change?' It's the most precise tool for identifying which components are on the critical path for a behavior.

clean_prompt = "The cat sat on the mat and the cat"
corrupted_prompt = "The cat sat on the mat and the dog"

clean_logits, clean_cache = model.run_with_cache(clean_tokens)
corrupted_logits, corrupted_cache = model.run_with_cache(corrupted_tokens)

# For each head, patch its output and measure effect
for layer in range(model.cfg.n_layers):
    for head in range(model.cfg.n_heads):
        def patch_hook(value, hook, l=layer, h=head):
            value[:, :, h, :] = corrupted_cache[hook.name][:, :, h, :]
            return value
        
        patched_logits = model.run_with_hooks(
            clean_tokens,
            fwd_hooks=[(f"blocks.{layer}.attn.hook_result", patch_hook)]
        )
        # Measure KL divergence from clean predictions
Heads with high KL divergence when patched are critical for the model's different behavior between the two inputs — they're on the repetition detection circuit.
Composition is how heads in different layers build on each other. Three types:
Q-composition: A's output → B's query ('A tells B what to look for')
K-composition: A's output → B's key ('A tells B what to advertise about this position')
V-composition: A's output → B's value ('A tells B what to copy when attention fires')

Direct Logit Attribution (DLA) measures each head's direct contribution to the model's output prediction — decomposing the final logits into per-component contributions through the unembedding matrix.

"I found that transformer attention heads in early layers implement approximate set-membership queries — essentially Bloom filters. When a token appears for the second time, these heads fire with high probability but also show a predictable false-positive rate that follows the classical Bloom filter capacity formula. They operate independently, and ablating them selectively degrades repetition handling while leaving novel-token processing intact."
The full replication has 7 steps. Here's the complete methodology:

Step 1 — Stimulus Generation: Three conditions to isolate repetition detection from semantic similarity. Exact repeats test the core mechanism, no-repeats give the baseline, and semantic near-misses test whether heads operate at token level vs meaning level.

# Semantic near-miss pairs (similar meaning, different tokens)
near_miss_pairs = [
    ("cat", "kitten"), ("dog", "puppy"), ("house", "home"),
    ("car", "vehicle"), ("ship", "boat"), ("stone", "rock"),
]

templates = [
    "The {w1} was near the {filler} and the {w2} was far away",
    "I saw a {w1} beside the {filler} but the {w2} had vanished",
    "A {w1} appeared behind the {filler} then the {w2} moved quickly",
]

Step 2 — Attention Extraction: For each stimulus, extract the attention weight from the second occurrence of the target to the first. This gives a per-head 'hit score' for repeats and a 'false alarm score' for non-repeats.
Step 3 — Per-Head Metrics: Three metrics that map directly onto Bloom filter properties:
Selectivity — signal-to-noise ratio (repeat attention / non-repeat attention)
Miss rate — false negatives (should be ≈ 0 for a Bloom filter)
False positive ratio — non-repeats that trigger above threshold
Step 4 — Head Identification: A head qualifies as a Bloom filter head if it has selectivity >3×, miss rate <10%, and mean hit attention >0.05.
Step 5 — Capacity Analysis: The classic Bloom filter formula predicts FP rate as a function of stored items:
FP_rate = (1 - e^(-k·n/m))^k where k = number of hash functions (≈ number of Bloom filter heads), n = items stored (≈ context length), m = bits in filter (≈ d_head = 64).

By varying the context size (n) and measuring observed FP rate, we can fit k and m. If the heads are truly Bloom filters, the curve should match the formula. The fitted capacity parameter m turns out to be in the same ballpark as d_head (64) — each head has 64 dimensions to 'store' its hash function.

Step 6 — Independence Test: Bloom filters with multiple hash functions require independence. We test with phi coefficients between heads' false-positive decisions. Low phi ≈ 0 confirms independent hash functions, meaning k heads give an effective FP rate of (individual FP)^k.

# For each pair of BF heads, compute phi on false positive decisions
for i, key_i in enumerate(bf_keys):
    for j, key_j in enumerate(bf_keys):
        d_i = head_decisions[key_i]
        d_j = head_decisions[key_j]
        
        n11 = sum(a and b for a, b in zip(d_i, d_j))
        n00 = sum(not a and not b for a, b in zip(d_i, d_j))
        n10 = sum(a and not b for a, b in zip(d_i, d_j))
        n01 = sum(not a and b for a, b in zip(d_i, d_j))
        
        denom = math.sqrt(max(n1x * n0x * nx1 * nx0, 1))
        phi = (n11 * n00 - n10 * n01) / denom
Step 7 — Ablation: The definitive causal test. Zero-ablate all Bloom filter heads and measure perplexity on repeated vs novel tokens.

1. STIMULUS GENERATION: 180 sentences across 3 conditions

2. HEAD IDENTIFICATION: ~4-6 heads meeting Bloom filter criteria
   L0H1:  selectivity=95.3x, miss=0%, FP=8%
   L0H5:  selectivity=24.6x, miss=0%, FP=12%
   L1H11: selectivity=29.9x, miss=2%, FP=6%
   L3H0:  selectivity=34.9x, miss=0%, FP=9%

3. CAPACITY: FP rate increases with context size, fitting formula
   k ≈ 4 heads, m ≈ 64 (= d_head)

4. INDEPENDENCE: Phi coefficients ≈ 0 between heads

5. ABLATION: Selective impact on repeat vs novel
   Repeat loss delta:  +0.15 (+9.7%)
   Novel loss delta:   -0.07 (-4.4%)
   Interaction:        +14.1%

CONCLUSION: These heads implement a probabilistic set-membership
data structure using QK circuits as hash functions.
Key questions and how to answer them using what you've learned:
"Walk me through your paper." → Steps 1-7 from Module 6, emphasizing the Bloom filter analogy at each step.
"How do you know it's not just copying?" → Semantic near-misses don't trigger (token-level matching). FP rate follows capacity formula (it's a hash, not a lookup).
"What's the causal evidence?" → Ablation shows selective impact on repeats. Path patching confirms heads are on the critical path.
"Why Bloom filter specifically?" → No false negatives ✓, yes false positives ✓, FP rate fits the formula ✓, multiple independent hash functions ✓, capacity scales with dimensionality ✓.

"What surprised you?" → How early (layers 0-3) this computation happens. How clean the independence is between heads. That a discrete CS data structure emerges from gradient descent on continuous weights.

questions.question: What does run_with_cache return that a normal forward pass doesn't?
options: Gradient information for all parameters
A cache of every intermediate activation at every hook point
The tokenized input as a separate tensor
A list of attention head names
questions.explanation: run_with_cache returns both the logits AND a ActivationCache object containing every intermediate tensor (attention patterns, residual streams, MLP outputs, etc.) at every hook point in the model.

questions.question: Why is mean ablation generally more principled than zero ablation?
options: It's faster to compute
Zero is never a valid activation value
Replacing with the mean stays closer to the training distribution that downstream layers expect
Mean ablation always gives larger effects
questions.explanation: Zero is out-of-distribution — downstream layers have never seen exactly-zero output from a head during training. Mean ablation replaces with the average activation, which is within the expected distribution and asks 'what if this head contributed nothing beyond its average?'

questions.question: What problem do Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) solve?
options: Making models run faster
Superposition — individual neurons are polysemantic, SAEs decompose into monosemantic features
Reducing model size for deployment
Fixing attention pattern errors
questions.explanation: SAEs address superposition: neurons respond to many unrelated concepts because the model packs more concepts than it has neurons. SAEs learn a larger dictionary of sparse, monosemantic features — each ideally corresponding to one interpretable concept.

questions.question: What is the key limitation of linear probes that ablation experiments address?
options: Probes are too slow to train
Probes can only find binary information
Probes can find information the model doesn't actually use — they're correlational, not causal
Probes require labeled data that doesn't exist
questions.explanation: A probe finding information encoded at some layer doesn't mean the model uses it. The info could be 'noise' that downstream computation ignores. Ablation provides the causal evidence — if removing a component changes behavior, it was being used.

questions.question: In the QK/OV framework, what does the QK circuit determine?
options: What information is copied when attention fires
The learning rate for the head
WHERE the head attends — which positions get high attention scores
How much the head contributes to the final output
questions.explanation: The QK circuit (W_Q^T × W_K) determines the attention pattern — which source positions each destination position attends to. The OV circuit separately determines WHAT information gets copied. This independence is what makes the framework so powerful.

questions.question: What is the key Bloom filter property that distinguishes it from exact lookup?
options: It's always correct
No false negatives, but non-zero false positives that follow a predictable capacity formula
It works on semantic similarity rather than exact matching
It requires multiple passes through the data
questions.explanation: A Bloom filter says 'definitely not in set' (no false negatives) or 'probably in set' (occasional false positives). The FP rate follows (1 - e^(-kn/m))^k. This is exactly what the attention heads show — and the near-miss test confirms it's token-level (not semantic) matching.

questions.question: Why do we test semantic near-misses (cat/kitten) in the Bloom filter replication?
options: To increase the training data size
To verify the heads operate at the TOKEN level, not the meaning level — ruling out semantic similarity
To test if the model understands synonyms
To calibrate the false positive threshold
questions.explanation: If the heads fired for cat→kitten, they'd be doing semantic similarity matching, not Bloom filtering. The fact that near-misses DON'T trigger confirms these heads match at the token level — exactly like a hash function, which is the core Bloom filter mechanism.
